g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of SQL Constraints. We will learn how to enforce data integrity in databases through various constraint types.
Database constraints are rules enforced by the DBMS that ensure data integrity and prevent invalid data from entering the database.
g.text: Constraints are declarative rules that specify conditions which data must satisfy.
They prevent duplicate records, orphaned data, and maintain consistency.
Enforced automatically by DBMS | Defined at column or table level | Reject invalid data automatically

g.data-narration: Without constraints, databases become vulnerable to data corruption, inconsistent records, and application failures due to invalid data entries.
g.text: ❌ Without constraints: Duplicate emails, invalid ages, orphaned records, data corruption
✓ With constraints: Valid data only, referential integrity, reliable applications
Constraints eliminate manual validation | Business rules enforced at database level | Cleaner application code

g.data-narration: The NOT NULL constraint ensures a column cannot contain NULL values, making fields mandatory for business operations like email or phone.
g.text: Ensures a column CANNOT contain NULL values. Fields become mandatory.

g.data-narration: The UNIQUE constraint ensures all values in a column differ from each other. Multiple UNIQUE constraints allowed per table, one NULL permitted.
g.text: Ensures all values in a column are DIFFERENT. Multiple UNIQUE allowed per table.

g.data-narration: The PRIMARY KEY uniquely identifies each row in a table. It cannot be NULL and only one per table is permitted, automatically indexed.
g.text: Uniquely identifies each row. Combines UNIQUE + NOT NULL. One per table.

g.data-narration: The FOREIGN KEY establishes relationships between tables, referencing the PRIMARY KEY in another table to ensure referential integrity.
g.text: Establishes parent-child relationship between tables. References PRIMARY KEY in another table.

g.data-narration: Foreign key actions define what happens to child records when parent data is modified: CASCADE deletes/updates children, SET NULL sets to NULL.
g.text: FOREIGN KEY Actions (ON DELETE / ON UPDATE)
Define behavior when referenced data is modified or deleted.

g.data-narration: The CHECK constraint specifies a condition that must be TRUE for each row, enforcing business rules like price > 0 or age >= 17.
g.text: Specifies a condition that must be TRUE for each row. Validates business rules at DB level.

g.data-narration: The DEFAULT constraint provides a fallback value when no value is specified during INSERT, useful for status fields or current dates.
g.text: Provides a default value when no value is specified during INSERT operations.

g.data-narration: Column-level constraints apply to single columns within the definition. Table-level constraints are defined after all columns and can span multiple columns.
Use ALTER TABLE to add or remove constraints from existing tables. ADD CONSTRAINT creates new rules, DROP CONSTRAINT removes them.
g.text: Adding Constraints to Existing Tables (ALTER TABLE)
Constraints can be added to or removed from existing tables using ALTER TABLE statements.
ALTER TABLE employees MODIFY salary DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL;

g.data-narration: Let's see a complete example: Student Information System with PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, and FOREIGN KEY constraints combined.
g.text: Combining multiple constraints in a real-world scenario.
dept_id INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES departments(dept_id)

g.data-narration: Remember: PRIMARY KEY is unique and not null (one per table), UNIQUE allows nulls (multiple per table), FOREIGN KEY links tables, CHECK validates conditions.
g.text:
CHECK
NOT NULL
One per table
Multiple allowed
Links tables
Condition TRUE
No NULLs

g.data-narration: Key exam tips: PRIMARY KEY cannot be NULL, CHECK validates expressions, create parent tables before child tables, name constraints explicitly for better error messages.
g.text: • PRIMARY KEY = UNIQUE + NOT NULL (one per table) | UNIQUE allows NULLs (multiple per table)
• FOREIGN KEY: Create parent table BEFORE child table | ON DELETE CASCADE/SET NULL/RESTRICT
• CHECK: Condition must be TRUE | Can reference multiple columns in same row
• DEFAULT provides value when none given | NOT NULL prohibits NULL completely
✓ Practice writing CREATE TABLE statements with various constraints!
